infobox.value: | [Development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development")  * [Producer](/wiki/Video_game_producer "Video game producer") * [Developer](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer")    + [Designer](/wiki/Video_game_design#Game_designer "Video game design")   + [Artist](/wiki/Game_art_design#Video_game_artist "Game art design")   + [Programmer](/wiki/Video_game_programmer "Video game programmer")  ---  * [Design](/wiki/Video_game_design "Video game design")   + [Level design](/wiki/Level_(video_games)#Level_design "Level (video games)") * [Programming](/wiki/Video_game_programming "Video game programming")   + [Engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine")   + [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games "Artificial intelligence in video games")   + [Creation system](/wiki/Game_creation_system "Game creation system") * [Graphics](/wiki/Video_game_graphics "Video game graphics") * [Music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music") * [Testing](/wiki/Game_testing "Game testing")

or **indie video game** (short for **independent video game**) is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") created by individuals or smaller [development teams](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer"), and typically without the financial and technical support of a large [game publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"), in contrast to most [AAA games](/wiki/AAA_games "AAA games"). Because of their independence and freedom to develop, indie games often focus on [innovation](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation"), experimental gameplay, and taking risks not usually afforded in AAA games. Indie games tend to be sold through [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") channels rather than at retail due to a lack of publisher support. The term is analogous to [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") or [independent film](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") in those respective mediums.

An **indie game** or **indie video game** (short for **independent video game**) is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") created by individuals or smaller [development teams](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer"), and typically without the financial and technical support of a large [game publisher](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"), in contrast to most [AAA games](/wiki/AAA_games "AAA games"). Because of their independence and freedom to develop, indie games often focus on [innovation](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation"), experimental gameplay, and taking risks not usually afforded in AAA games. Indie games tend to be sold through [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") channels rather than at retail due to a lack of publisher support. The term is analogous to [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") or [independent film](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") in those respective mediums.

Indie game development bore out from the same concepts of amateur and hobbyist programming that grew with the introduction of the [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") and the simple [BASIC](/wiki/BASIC "BASIC") computer language in the 1970s and 1980s. So-called bedroom coders, particularly in the United Kingdom and other parts of Europe, made their own games and used [mail order](/wiki/Mail_order "Mail order") to distribute their products; they later shifted to other software distribution methods with the onset of the Internet in the 1990s, such as [shareware](/wiki/Shareware "Shareware") and other [file sharing](/wiki/File_sharing "File sharing") distribution methods. However, by this time, interest in hobbyist programming had waned due to rising costs of development and competition from video game publishers and home consoles.

The modern take on the indie game scene resulted from a combination of numerous factors in the early 2000s, including technical, economic, and social concepts that made indie games less expensive to make and distribute but more visible to larger audiences and offered non-traditional gameplay from the current mainstream games. A number of indie games at that time became success stories that drove more interest in the area. New industry opportunities have arisen since then, including new digital storefronts, crowdfunding, and other indie funding mechanisms to help new teams get their games off the ground. There are also low-cost and [open-source development](/wiki/Open-source_software_development "Open-source software development") tools available for smaller teams across all gaming platforms, boutique indie game publishers that leave creative freedom to the developers, and industry recognition of indie games alongside mainstream ones at major game award events.

Around 2015, the increasing number of indie games being published led to fears of an "[indiepocalypse](/wiki/Indiepocalypse "Indiepocalypse")", referring to an oversupply of games that would make the entire market unprofitable. Although the market did not collapse, discoverability remains an issue for most indie developers, with many games not being financially profitable. Examples of successful indie games include *[Cave Story](/wiki/Cave_Story "Cave Story")*, *[Braid](/wiki/Braid_(video_game) "Braid (video game)")*, *[Super Meat Boy](/wiki/Super_Meat_Boy "Super Meat Boy")*, *[Terraria](/wiki/Terraria "Terraria")*, *[Fez](/wiki/Fez_(video_game) "Fez (video game)")*, *[Hotline Miami](/wiki/Hotline_Miami "Hotline Miami")*, *[Shovel Knight](/wiki/Shovel_Knight "Shovel Knight")*, *[Hollow Knight](/wiki/Hollow_Knight "Hollow Knight")*, and *[Undertale](/wiki/Undertale "Undertale")*. Other indie games have become multimedia franchises due to their success including *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*, *[Five Nights at Freddy's](/wiki/Five_Nights_at_Freddy%27s "Five Nights at Freddy's")*, *[Cuphead](/wiki/Cuphead "Cuphead")*, and *[Among Us](/wiki/Among_Us "Among Us")*.

Other indie games have been recognized as some of the best games of all time, including *[Hades](/wiki/Hades_(video_game) "Hades (video game)")* and  *[Balatro](/wiki/Balatro "Balatro")*, while others have established new video game genres, including *[Slay the Spire](/wiki/Slay_the_Spire "Slay the Spire")* and *[Vampire Survivors](/wiki/Vampire_Survivors "Vampire Survivors")*.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

The term "indie game" itself is based on similar terms like [independent film](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") and [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music"), where the concept is often related to self-publishing and independence from major studios or distributors.[[1]](#cite_note-eurog_whatisindie-1) However, as with both indie films and music, there is no exact, widely accepted definition of what constitutes an "indie game" besides falling well outside the bounds of [triple-A video game development](/wiki/AAA_(video_gaming) "AAA (video gaming)") by large publishers and development studios.[[2]](#cite_note-IGMWhatIsIndie-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[4]](#cite_note-GameTunnelUnderstanding-4)[[5]](#cite_note-IGNCategoryDelusion-5) One simple definition, described by Laura Parker for *[GameSpot](/wiki/GameSpot "GameSpot")*, says "independent video game development is the business of making games without the support of publishers", but this does not cover all situations.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) Dan Pearce of *[IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN")* stated that the only consensus for what constitutes an indie game is a "[I know it when I see it](/wiki/I_know_it_when_I_see_it "I know it when I see it")"-type assessment, since no single definition can capture what games are broadly considered indie.[[7]](#cite_note-ign_indie_def-7)

Indie games generally share certain common characteristics. One method to define an indie game is the nature of independence, which can either be:[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)

* Financial independence: In such situations, the developers have paid for the development and/or publication of the game themselves or from other funding sources such as [crowd funding](/wiki/Crowd_funding "Crowd funding"), and specifically without financial support of a large publisher.
* Independence of thought: In this case, the developers crafted their game without any oversight or directional influence by a third party such as a publisher.

Another means to evaluate a game as indie is to examine its development team, with indie games being developed by individuals, small teams, or small independent companies that are often specifically formed for the development of one specific game.[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)[[9]](#cite_note-GameTunnelInnovation-9) Typically, indie games are *smaller* than mainstream titles.[[9]](#cite_note-GameTunnelInnovation-9) Indie game developers are generally not financially backed by [video game publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"), who are risk-averse and prefer "big-budget games".[[10]](#cite_note-Prince2004-10) Instead, indie game developers usually have smaller budgets, usually sourcing from personal funds or via [crowdfunding](/wiki/Crowdfunding "Crowdfunding").[[2]](#cite_note-IGMWhatIsIndie-2)[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[5]](#cite_note-IGNCategoryDelusion-5)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-GameSpotRiseOfIndieDev-12) Being independent, developers do not have controlling interests[[4]](#cite_note-GameTunnelUnderstanding-4) or creative limitations,[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[13]](#cite_note-JoystiqBeingSuccessful-13)[[5]](#cite_note-IGNCategoryDelusion-5) and do not require the approval of a publisher,[[2]](#cite_note-IGMWhatIsIndie-2) as mainstream game developers usually do.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBethke2003102-14) Design decisions are thus also not limited by an allocated budget.[[13]](#cite_note-JoystiqBeingSuccessful-13) Furthermore, smaller team sizes increase individual involvement.[[15]](#cite_note-MCVMoreCreative-15)

However, this view is not all-encompassing, as there are numerous cases of games where development is not independent of a major publisher but still considered by some people to be indie.[[1]](#cite_note-eurog_whatisindie-1) Some notable instances of games include:

* *[Journey](/wiki/Journey_(2012_video_game) "Journey (2012 video game)")* was created by [thatgamecompany](/wiki/Thatgamecompany "Thatgamecompany"), but had financial backing of [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") as well as publishing support. [Kellee Santiago](/wiki/Kellee_Santiago "Kellee Santiago") of thatgamecompany believes that they are an independent studio because they were able to innovate on their game without Sony's involvement.[[1]](#cite_note-eurog_whatisindie-1)
* *[Bastion](/wiki/Bastion_(video_game) "Bastion (video game)")*, similarly, was developed by [Supergiant Games](/wiki/Supergiant_Games "Supergiant Games"), but with publishing by [Warner Bros. Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Entertainment "Warner Bros. Entertainment"), primarily to avoid difficulties with the certification process on [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Greg Kasavin of Supergiant notes they consider their studio indie as they lack any parent company.[[1]](#cite_note-eurog_whatisindie-1)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* *[The Witness](/wiki/The_Witness_(2016_video_game) "The Witness (2016 video game)")* was developed by [Jonathan Blow](/wiki/Jonathan_Blow "Jonathan Blow") and his studio Thekla, Inc. Though self-funded and published, the game's development cost around $6 million and was priced at $40, in contrast to most indie games typically priced up to $20. Blow believed this type of game represented something between indie and AAA publishing.[[18]](#cite_note-engadget_jan16-18)
* *[No Man's Sky](/wiki/No_Man%27s_Sky "No Man's Sky")* was developed by [Hello Games](/wiki/Hello_Games "Hello Games"), though with publishing but non-financial support from Sony; on release, the game had a price equal to a typical AAA title. Sean Murray of Hello Games believes that because they are still a small team and the game is highly experimental that they consider themselves indie.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* *[Dave the Diver](/wiki/Dave_the_Diver "Dave the Diver")* was developed by Mintrocket, a thirty-person studio owned by [Nexon](/wiki/Nexon "Nexon"). Despite this corporate ownership, and the studio itself stating they do not consider themselves as an indie studio,[[20]](#cite_note-20) the game's approach was considered less traditional as to be considered an indie game by the industry, including being nominated for Best Indie Game at [The Game Awards 2023](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2023 "The Game Awards 2023").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* *[Clair Obscur: Expedition 33](/wiki/Clair_Obscur:_Expedition_33 "Clair Obscur: Expedition 33")* was similarly nominated for and won both indie game awards at [The Game Awards 2025](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2025 "The Game Awards 2025"). Further, while its developer, Sandfall Interactive, was a small independent team of 30 alongside outsourced work, the game was more frequently compared to AA or AAA games due to its budget and inclusion of high-profile voice actors through funding from the publisher [Kepler Interactive](/wiki/Kepler_Interactive "Kepler Interactive").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Nidhogg_video_game_screenshot_1.png/250px-Nidhogg_video_game_screenshot_1.png)](/wiki/File:Nidhogg_video_game_screenshot_1.png)

*[Nidhogg](/wiki/Nidhogg_(video_game) "Nidhogg (video game)")* is an example of an indie game that relies on a retro pixel-based 1980s style for its presentation.

Yet another angle to evaluate a game as indie is from its innovation, creativity, and artistic experimentation, factors enabled by small teams free of financial and creative oversight. This definition is reflective of an "indie spirit" that is diametrically opposite of the corporate culture of AAA development, and makes a game "indie", where the factors of financial and creative independence make a game "independent".[[27]](#cite_note-every_game_indie-27)[[2]](#cite_note-IGMWhatIsIndie-2)[[9]](#cite_note-GameTunnelInnovation-9)[[15]](#cite_note-MCVMoreCreative-15)[[28]](#cite_note-GamasutraFuture-28)[[29]](#cite_note-GamasutraMovement-29)[[30]](#cite_note-McGuire_2009_27-30)[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31) Developers with limited ability to create graphics can rely on [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay") innovation.[[32]](#cite_note-ForbesIndieDevsRiseUp-32) This often leads to indie games having a [retro style](/wiki/Retrogaming "Retrogaming") of the [8-bit](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles") and [16-bit generations](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles"), with simpler graphics atop the more complex mechanics.[[27]](#cite_note-every_game_indie-27) Indie games may fall into classic game genres, but new gameplay innovations have been seen.[[29]](#cite_note-GamasutraMovement-29) However, being "indie" does not imply that the game focuses on innovation.[[9]](#cite_note-GameTunnelInnovation-9)[[33]](#cite_note-33) In fact, many games with the "indie" label can be of poor quality and may not be made for profit.[[5]](#cite_note-IGNCategoryDelusion-5)

[Jesper Juul](/wiki/Jesper_Juul_(game_researcher) "Jesper Juul (game researcher)"), an associate professor at [The Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/The_Royal_Danish_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "The Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts") that has studied the video game market, wrote in his book *Handmade Pixels* that the definition of an indie game is vague, and depends on different subjective considerations. Juul classified three ways games can be considered indie: those that are financially independent of large publishers, those that are aesthetically independent of and significantly different from the mainstream art and visual styles used in AAA games, and those that present cultural ideas that are independent from mainstream games. Juul however wrote that ultimately the labeling of a game as "indie" still can be highly subjective and no single rule helps delineate indie games from non-indie ones.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Games that are not as large as most triple-A games, but are developed by larger independent studios with or without publisher backing and that can apply triple-A design principles and polish due to the experience of the team, have sometimes been called "triple-I" games, reflecting the middle ground between these extremes. [Ninja Theory](/wiki/Ninja_Theory "Ninja Theory")'s *[Hellblade: Senua's Sacrifice](/wiki/Hellblade:_Senua%27s_Sacrifice "Hellblade: Senua's Sacrifice")* is considered a prime example of a triple-I game.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) A further distinction from indie games are those considered double-A ("AA"), tending to be from mid to large-size studios ranging from 50 to 100 team members and larger than typically associated with indie games, that often work under similar practices as triple-A studios but still retain creative control of their titles from a publisher.[[7]](#cite_note-ign_indie_def-7)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Indie games are distinct from [open source games](/wiki/Open-source_video_game "Open-source video game"). The latter are games which are developed with the intent to release the [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") and other assets under an [open source license](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software"). While many of the same principles used to develop open source games are the same as for indie games, open source games are not developed for commercial gain and instead as a hobbyist pursuit.[[38]](#cite_note-38) However, commercial sales are not a requirement for an indie game and such games can be offered as [freeware](/wiki/Freeware "Freeware"), most notably with *[Spelunky](/wiki/Spelunky "Spelunky")* on its original release and *[Dwarf Fortress](/wiki/Dwarf_Fortress "Dwarf Fortress")*, with the exception of its enhanced visual front-end version while its base version remains free.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The onset of indie game development is difficult to track due to the broadness of what defines an indie game, and the term was not really in use until the early 2000s.[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) Until the 2000s, other terms like amateur, enthusiast, and hobbyist software or games were used to describe such software.[[41]](#cite_note-parker_digra_2013-41) Today, terms like amateur and hobbyist development are more reflective of those that create [mods](/wiki/Mod_(computer_gaming) "Mod (computer gaming)") for existing games,[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-42) or work with specific technologies or game parts rather than the development of full games.[[4]](#cite_note-GameTunnelUnderstanding-4) Such hobbyists usually produce [non-commercial](/wiki/Non-commercial "Non-commercial") products and may range from novices to industry veterans.[[4]](#cite_note-GameTunnelUnderstanding-4)

### Before home computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Before home computers")]

There is some debate as to whether independent game development started prior to the 1977 [home computer](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") revolution with games developed for [mainframe computers](/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer") at universities and other large institutions. 1962's *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* was not commercially financed and was made by a small team, but there was no commercial sector of the video game industry at that time to distinguish from independent works.[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43)

[Joyce Weisbecker](/wiki/Joyce_Weisbecker "Joyce Weisbecker"), who considers herself the first indie designer, created several games for the [RCA Studio II](/wiki/RCA_Studio_II "RCA Studio II") home console in 1976 as an independent contractor for [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation").[[44]](#cite_note-edwards20171027-44)

### Home computers (late 1970s-1980s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Home computers (late 1970s-1980s)")]

When the first personal computers were released in 1977, they each included a pre-installed version of the [BASIC](/wiki/BASIC "BASIC") computer language along with example programs, including games, to show what users could do with these systems. The availability of BASIC led to people trying to make their own programs. Sales of the 1978 rerelease of the book *[BASIC Computer Games](/wiki/BASIC_Computer_Games "BASIC Computer Games")* by [David H. Ahl](/wiki/David_H._Ahl "David H. Ahl") that included the [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") for over one hundred games, eventually surpassed over one million copies.[[45]](#cite_note-time_basic-45) The availability of BASIC inspired a number of people to start writing their own games.[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/ZXSpectrum48k.jpg/250px-ZXSpectrum48k.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZXSpectrum48k.jpg)

The 1982 [ZX Spectrum](/wiki/ZX_Spectrum "ZX Spectrum") was popular with hobbyist programmers in the UK.

Many personal computer games written by individuals or two person teams were self-distributed in stores or sold through [mail order](/wiki/Mail_order "Mail order").[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) [Atari, Inc.](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.") launched the [Atari Program Exchange](/wiki/Atari_Program_Exchange "Atari Program Exchange") in 1981 to publish user-written software, including games, for [Atari 8-bit computers](/wiki/Atari_8-bit_computers "Atari 8-bit computers").[[46]](#cite_note-apx1981summer-46) Print magazines such as *[SoftSide](/wiki/SoftSide "SoftSide")*, *[Compute!](/wiki/Compute! "Compute!")*, and *[Antic](/wiki/Antic_(magazine) "Antic (magazine)")* solicited games from hobbyists, written in BASIC or [assembly language](/wiki/Assembly_language "Assembly language"), to publish as [type-in listings](/wiki/Type-in_listing "Type-in listing").

In the United Kingdom, early microcomputers such as the [ZX Spectrum](/wiki/ZX_Spectrum "ZX Spectrum") were popular, launching a range of "bedroom coders" which initiated the UK's video game industry.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-e&p_uk_history-48) During this period, the idea that indie games could provide experimental gameplay concepts or demonstrate niche [arthouse](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") appeal had been established.[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43) Many games from the bedroom coders of the United Kingdom, such as *[Manic Miner](/wiki/Manic_Miner "Manic Miner")* (1983), incorporated the quirkiness of [British humour](/wiki/British_humour "British humour") and made them highly experimental games.[[49]](#cite_note-wired_uk_history-49)[[50]](#cite_note-eurogamer_manic_miner-50) Other games like *[Alien Garden](/wiki/Alien_Garden "Alien Garden")* (1982) showed highly-experimental gameplay.[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43) [Infocom](/wiki/Infocom "Infocom") itself advertised its text-based [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction") games by emphasizing their lack of graphics in lieu of the players' imagination, at a time that graphics-heavy action games were commonplace.[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43)

### Shareware and chasing the console (1990s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Shareware and chasing the console (1990s)")]

By the mid-1990s, the recognition of the personal computer as a viable gaming option, and advances in technology that led to [3D gaming](/wiki/Video_game_graphics#3D "Video game graphics") created many commercial opportunities for video games. During the last part of the 1990s, visibility of games from these single or small team studios scene waned, since a small team could not readily compete in costs, speed and distribution as a commercial entity could. The industry had started to coalesce around [video game publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher") that could pay larger developers to make games and handle all the marketing and publication costs as well as opportunities to franchise game series.[[48]](#cite_note-e&p_uk_history-48) Publishers tended to be risk averse due to high costs of production, and they would reject all small-size and too innovative concepts of small game developers.[[10]](#cite_note-Prince2004-10) The market also became fractured due to the prevalence of video game consoles, which required expensive or difficult-to-acquire [game development kits](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit") typically reserved for larger developers and publishers.[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43)

There were still significant developments from smaller teams that laid the basis of indie games going forward. [Shareware](/wiki/Shareware "Shareware") games became a popular means to distribute demos or partially complete games in the 1980s and into the 1990s, where players could purchase the full game from the vendor after trying it. As such demos were generally free to distribute, shareware demo compilations would frequently be included in gaming magazines at that time, providing an easy means for amateur and hobbyist developers to be recognized. The ability to produce numerous copies of games, even if just shareware/demo versions, at a low cost helped to propel the idea as the PC as a gaming platform.[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31)[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) At the time, shareware was generally associated with hobbyist programmers, but the releases of *[Wolfenstein 3D](/wiki/Wolfenstein_3D "Wolfenstein 3D")* in 1992 and *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")* in 1993 showed the shareware route to be a viable platform for titles from mainstream developers.[[43]](#cite_note-polygon_handmade_pixels-43)

### Rise of indie games from digital distribution (2000−2005)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rise of indie games from digital distribution (2000−2005)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Screenshot_from_Metanet_Software_game%2C_N_-_27.jpg/250px-Screenshot_from_Metanet_Software_game%2C_N_-_27.jpg)](/wiki/File:Screenshot_from_Metanet_Software_game,_N_-_27.jpg)

*[N](/wiki/N_(video_game) "N (video game)")* is a 2004 [browser game](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") that later was developed into a commercial indie game, *[N++](/wiki/N%2B%2B "N++")* in 2015.

The current, common understanding of indie games on personal computer took shape in the early 2000s from several factors. Key was the availability of [online distribution](/wiki/Online_distribution "Online distribution") over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), allowing game developers to sell directly to players and bypassing limitations of retail distribution and the need for a publisher.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIuppaBorst200910-52)[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) Software technologies used to drive the growth of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"), like [Adobe Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash"), were available at low cost to developers, and provided another means for indie games to grow.[[32]](#cite_note-ForbesIndieDevsRiseUp-32)[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40)[[53]](#cite_note-53) The new interest in indie games led to [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware") and [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine") developers to offer their products at low or no cost for indie development,[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) in addition to [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") libraries and engines.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Dedicated software like [GameMaker Studio](/wiki/GameMaker_Studio "GameMaker Studio") and tools for unified game engines like [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(game_engine) "Unity (game engine)") and [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") removed much of the programming barriers needed for a prospective indie developer to create these games.[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) The commercial possibilities for indie games at this point helped to distinguish these games from any prior amateur game.[[41]](#cite_note-parker_digra_2013-41)

There were other shifts in the commercial environment that were seen as drivers for the rise of indie games in the 2000s. Many of the games to be indie games of this period were considered to be the antithesis of mainstream games and which highlighted the independence of how these games were made compared to the collective of mainstream titles. Many of them took a retro-style approach to their design, art, or other factors in development, such as *[Cave Story](/wiki/Cave_Story "Cave Story")* in 2004, which proved popular with players.[[41]](#cite_note-parker_digra_2013-41)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Social and political changes also led to the use of indie games not only for entertainment purposes but to also tell a message related to these factors, something that could not be done in mainstream titles.[[41]](#cite_note-parker_digra_2013-41) In comparing indie games to [independent film](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") and the state of their respective industries, the indie game's rise was occurring approximately at the same relative time as its market was starting to grow exponentially and be seen as a supporting offshoot of the mainstream works.[[41]](#cite_note-parker_digra_2013-41)

### Shifting industry and increased visibility (2005−2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Shifting industry and increased visibility (2005−2014)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Fez_%28video_game%29_screenshot_08.png/250px-Fez_%28video_game%29_screenshot_08.png)](/wiki/File:Fez_(video_game)_screenshot_08.png)

*[Fez](/wiki/Fez_(video_game) "Fez (video game)")* was one of several indie games highlighted in *[Indie Game: The Movie](/wiki/Indie_Game:_The_Movie "Indie Game: The Movie")* as indie games entered mainstream coverage.

Indie games saw a large boost in visibility within the video game industry and the rest of the world starting around 2005. A key driver was the transition into new digital distribution methods with storefronts like [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") that offered indie games alongside traditional AAA titles, as well as specialized storefronts for indie games. While direct online distribution helped indie games to reach players, these storefronts allowed developers to publish, update, and advertise their games directly, and players to download the games anywhere, with the storefront otherwise handling the distribution and sales factors.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-42)[[32]](#cite_note-ForbesIndieDevsRiseUp-32)[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[29]](#cite_note-GamasutraMovement-29)[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31) While Steam itself initially began heavy curation, it eventually allowed for indie publishing with its Steam Greenlight and Steam Direct programs, vastly increasing the number of games available.[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40)

Further indie game growth in this period came from the departure of large publishers like [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") and [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") from their smaller, one-off titles to focus on their larger, more successful properties, leaving the indie game space to provide shorter and more experimental titles as alternatives.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Costs of developing AAA games had risen greatly, to an average cost of tens of millions of dollars in 2007–2008 per title, and there was little room for risks in gameplay experimentation.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Another driver came from discussions related to whether [video games could be seen as an art form](/wiki/Video_games_as_an_art_form "Video games as an art form"); movie critic [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") postulated in open debates that video games could not be art in 2005 and 2006, leading to developers creating indie games to specifically challenge that notion.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Indie video game development saw a further boost by the use of [crowdfunding](/wiki/Crowdfunding "Crowdfunding") as a means for indie developers to raise funds to produce a game and to determine the desire for a game, rather than risk time and investment into a game that does not sell well. While video games had used crowdfunding prior to 2012, several [large indie game-related projects](/wiki/Crowdfunding_in_video_games "Crowdfunding in video games") successfully raised millions of dollars through [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter"), and since then, several other similar crowdfunding options for game developers have become available. Crowdfunding eliminated some of the cost risk associated with indie game development, and created more opportunities for indie developers to take chances on new titles.[[40]](#cite_note-pcgamer_indie-40) With more indie titles emerging during this period, larger publishers and the industry as a whole started taking notice of indie games as a significant movement within the field. One of the first examples of this was *[World of Goo](/wiki/World_of_Goo "World of Goo")* (2008), whose developers 2D Boy had tried but failed to gain any publisher support prior to release. On release, the game was recognized at various award events including the [Independent Games Festival](/wiki/Independent_Games_Festival "Independent Games Festival"), leading to publishers that had previously rejected World of Goo to offer to publish it.[[59]](#cite_note-vg_goo-59) The success of indie video games on crowdfunding platforms also inspired a wave of [indie tabletop role-playing game](/wiki/Indie_role-playing_game "Indie role-playing game") developers to follow the same business model.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Console manufacturers also helped increase recognition of indie games in this period. By the [seventh generation](/wiki/Seventh_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Seventh generation of video game consoles") of consoles in 2005, each platform provided online services for players–namely [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live"), [PlayStation Network](/wiki/PlayStation_Network "PlayStation Network"), and [Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection](/wiki/Nintendo_Wi-Fi_Connection "Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection")–which included digital game distribution. Following the increased popularity of indie games on computers, these services started publishing them alongside larger releases.[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3)[[30]](#cite_note-McGuire_2009_27-30) The [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360") had launched in 2005 with [Xbox Live Arcade](/wiki/Xbox_Live_Arcade "Xbox Live Arcade") (XBLA), a service that included some indie games, though these drew little attention in the first few years. In 2008, Microsoft ran its "XBLA Summer of Arcade" promotion, which included the releases of indie games *[Braid](/wiki/Braid_(video_game) "Braid (video game)")*, *[Castle Crashers](/wiki/Castle_Crashers "Castle Crashers")*, and *[Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved 2](/wiki/Geometry_Wars:_Retro_Evolved_2 "Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved 2")* alongside two AAA games. While all three indie games had a high number of downloads, *Braid* received critical acclaim and drew mainstream media recognition for being a game developed by two people.[[62]](#cite_note-indies_on_consoles-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Microsoft continued to follow up on this promotion in the following years, bringing in more games onto XBLA such as *[Super Meat Boy](/wiki/Super_Meat_Boy "Super Meat Boy")*, *[Limbo](/wiki/Limbo_(video_game) "Limbo (video game)")*, and *[Fez](/wiki/Fez_(video_game) "Fez (video game)")*.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) Sony and Nintendo followed suit, encouraging indie developers to bring games onto their platforms.[[62]](#cite_note-indies_on_consoles-62) By 2013, all three console manufacturers had established programs that allowed indie developers to apply for low-cost development toolkits and licenses to publish directly onto the console's respective storefronts following approval processes.[[62]](#cite_note-indies_on_consoles-62) A number of "boutique" indie game publishers were founded in this period to support funding, technical support, and publishing of indie games across various digital and retail platforms.[[66]](#cite_note-gibiz_boutique-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) In 2012, [*Journey*](/wiki/Journey_(2012_video_game) "Journey (2012 video game)") became the first Indie game to win the [Game Developers Choice Award for Game of the Year](/wiki/Game_Developers_Choice_Award_for_Game_of_the_Year "Game Developers Choice Award for Game of the Year") and [D.I.C.E. Award for Game of the Year](/wiki/D.I.C.E._Award_for_Game_of_the_Year "D.I.C.E. Award for Game of the Year").[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Several other indie games were released during this period to critical and/or commercial success.[[70]](#cite_note-:0-70) *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")* (2011), the [best-selling video game of all time](/wiki/Best-selling_game_of_all_time "Best-selling game of all time") as of 2024,[[71]](#cite_note-71) was originally released as an indie game[[72]](#cite_note-72) before its developer [Mojang Studios](/wiki/Mojang_Studios "Mojang Studios") was acquired by [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") in 2014 and brought into [Xbox Game Studios](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios").[[73]](#cite_note-73) Another indie game, *[Terraria](/wiki/Terraria "Terraria")*, was released that same year and has become the eighth best selling video game of all time,[[74]](#cite_note-74) as well the highest rated game on Steam as of 2022.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Other successful indie games released during this time include *[The Binding of Isaac](/wiki/The_Binding_of_Isaac_(video_game) "The Binding of Isaac (video game)")* (2011),[[76]](#cite_note-76) *[Hotline Miami](/wiki/Hotline_Miami "Hotline Miami")* (2012),[[77]](#cite_note-77) *[Shovel Knight](/wiki/Shovel_Knight "Shovel Knight")* (2014),[[78]](#cite_note-78) and *[Five Nights at Freddy's](/wiki/Five_Nights_at_Freddy%27s_(video_game) "Five Nights at Freddy's (video game)")* (2014).[[79]](#cite_note-:022-79) *Hotline Miami* inspired many to begin developing games[[80]](#cite_note-80) and contributed to the rise in indie game released during this time period,[[81]](#cite_note-81) while *Shovel Knight* and *Five Nights at Freddy's* spawned successful media franchises, with the latter becoming a cultural phenomenon.[[79]](#cite_note-:022-79)[[82]](#cite_note-82) [Mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games") also became popular with indie developers, with inexpensive development tools and low-barrier storefronts with the [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)") and [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") opening in the late 2000s.[[83]](#cite_note-poly_too_many_games-83) In 2012, a documentary, *[Indie Game: The Movie](/wiki/Indie_Game:_The_Movie "Indie Game: The Movie")*, was created that covers several successful games from this period.[[70]](#cite_note-:0-70)

### Fears regarding saturation and discoverability (2015−present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Fears regarding saturation and discoverability (2015−present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Steam_game_count_chart.png/960px-Steam_game_count_chart.png)](/wiki/File:Steam_game_count_chart.png)

The number of games, by year, published on Steam, estimated by [Steam Spy](/wiki/Steam_Spy "Steam Spy") in January 2020.[[84]](#cite_note-sales_by_year-84)[[85]](#cite_note-pcgi.biz_27k-85)[[86]](#cite_note-pcgi.biz_34k-86) The years 2004 and 2005—with seven and six games, respectively—are not visible on this chart.

Leading into 2015, there was concern that the rise of easy-to-use tools to create and distribute video games could lead to an oversupply of video games, which was termed the "indiepocalypse".[[87]](#cite_note-87) This perception of an indiepocalypse is not unanimous; [Jeff Vogel](/wiki/Jeff_Vogel "Jeff Vogel") stated in a talk at [GDC 2016](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") that any downturn was just part of the standard [business cycle](/wiki/Business_cycle "Business cycle"). The size of the indie game market was estimated in March 2016 to be at least $1 billion per year for just those games offered through [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)").[[88]](#cite_note-gdc_2016-88) Mike Wilson, Graeme Struthers and Harry Miller, the co-founders of indie publisher [Devolver Digital](/wiki/Devolver_Digital "Devolver Digital"), stated in April 2016 that the market in indie games is more competitive than ever but continues to appear healthy with no signs of faltering.[[89]](#cite_note-89) *[Gamasutra](/wiki/Gamasutra "Gamasutra")* said that by the end of 2016, while there had not be any type of catastrophic collapse of the indie game market, there were signs that the growth of the market had significantly slowed and that it has entered a "post-indiepocalypse" phase as business models related to indie games adjust to these new market conditions.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

While there has not been any type of collapse of the indie game field since 2015, there are concerns that the market is far too large for many developers to get noticed. Very few selected indie titles get wide coverage in the media, and are typically referred to as "indie darlings". In some cases, indie darlings are identified through consumer reactions that praise the game rather than direct industry influence, leading to further coverage; examples of such games include *[Celeste](/wiki/Celeste_(video_game) "Celeste (video game)")* and *[Untitled Goose Game](/wiki/Untitled_Goose_Game "Untitled Goose Game")*.[[91]](#cite_note-:4-91) However, there are also times where the video game media may see a future title as a success and position it as an indie darling before its release, only to have the game fail to make a strong impression on players, such as in the case of *[No Man's Sky](/wiki/No_Man%27s_Sky "No Man's Sky")* and *[Where the Water Tastes Like Wine](/wiki/Where_the_Water_Tastes_Like_Wine "Where the Water Tastes Like Wine")*.[[91]](#cite_note-:4-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Discoverability has become an issue for indie developers as well. With the [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") distribution service allowing any developer to offer their game with minimal cost to them, there are thousands of games being added each year, and developers have come to rely heavily on Steam's discovery tools – methods to tailor catalog pages to customers based on past purchases – to help sell their titles.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Mobile app stores have had similar problems with large volumes of offers but poor means for discovery by consumers in the late 2010s.[[83]](#cite_note-poly_too_many_games-83) Several indie developers have found it critical to have a good [public relations](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations") campaign across social media and to interact with the press to make sure a game is noticed early on in its development cycle to get interest and maintain that interest through release, which adds to costs of development.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Several games during this time have still seen success, including games that were referred to as "indie darlings."[[91]](#cite_note-:4-91) Some of the most popular indie games from this time were primarily popularized over social media and spawned cultural phenomena, such as *[Undertale](/wiki/Undertale "Undertale")* (2015) and *[Among Us](/wiki/Among_Us "Among Us")* (2018),[[96]](#cite_note-npr-96)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) with the latter being one of the most popular games during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") in 2020 and 2021 with half a billion players.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) A similar example is *[Lethal Company](/wiki/Lethal_Company "Lethal Company")*, which was released into early access in 2023 and was popularized through internet culture, becoming one of the most played games of the year.[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3) More commercially successful games from this time include *[Enter the Gungeon](/wiki/Enter_the_Gungeon "Enter the Gungeon")*,[[97]](#cite_note-97) *[Stardew Valley](/wiki/Stardew_Valley "Stardew Valley")*,[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) *[Hollow Knight](/wiki/Hollow_Knight "Hollow Knight")*,[[98]](#cite_note-98) and *[Cuphead](/wiki/Cuphead "Cuphead")*.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Other regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other regions")]

Indie games are generally associated with Western regions, specifically with North American, European, and Oceanic areas. However, other countries have had similar expansions of indie games that have intersected with the global industry.

#### Japanese *doujin* soft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Japanese doujin soft")]

Main article: [Doujin soft](/wiki/Doujin_soft "Doujin soft")

In Japan, the doujin soft community has generally been treated as a hobbyist activity up through the 2010s. Computers and bedroom coding had taken off similarly in the late 1970s and early 1980s, but the computer market was quickly overwhelmed by consoles. Still, hobbyist programmers continued to develop games. One area that Japan had focused on were [game development kits](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit"), specialized software that would allow users to create their own games. A key line of these were produced by [ASCII Corporation](/wiki/ASCII_Corporation "ASCII Corporation"), which published *[ASCII](/wiki/ASCII_(magazine) "ASCII (magazine)")*, a hobbyist programming magazine that users could share their programs with. Over time, ASCII saw the opportunity to publish game development kits, and by 1992, released the first commercial version of the *[RPG Maker](/wiki/RPG_Maker "RPG Maker")* software. While the software cost money to obtain, users could release completed games with it as freeware or commercial products, which established the potential for a commercial independent games market by the early 2000s, aligning with the popularity of indie games in the West.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Like other Japanese fan-created works in other media, doujin games were often built from existing assets and did not receive much respect or interest from consumers, and instead were generally made to be played and shared with other interested players and at conventions. Around 2013, market forces began to shift with the popularity of indie games in the Western regions, bringing more interest to doujin games as legitimate titles. The [Tokyo Game Show](/wiki/Tokyo_Game_Show "Tokyo Game Show") first offered a special area for doujin games in 2013 with support from [Sony Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment") who had been a promoter of Western indie games in prior years, and has expanded that since.[[101]](#cite_note-Ellison-101) The distinction between Japanese-developed doujin games and indie games is ambiguous - the use of the term usually refers to if their popularity formed in Western or Eastern markets before the mid-2010s, and if they are made with the aim of selling large copies or just as a passion project; the long-running [bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell") *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")* series, developed entirely by one-man independent developer [ZUN](/wiki/ZUN_(video_game_developer) "ZUN (video game developer)") since 1996, has been called both indie and doujin.[[102]](#cite_note-pcgamerstory-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) Meanwhile, despite being Japanese-developed, *Cave Story* is primarily referred to as an "indie game" because of its success in the Western market. It is one of the most influential indie games, also contributing to the resurgence of the [Metroidvania](/wiki/Metroidvania "Metroidvania") genre.[[101]](#cite_note-Ellison-101)[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-Nutt-105) Doujin games also got a strong interest in Western markets after some English-speaking groups translated various titles with permission for English release, most notably with *[Recettear: An Item Shop's Tale](/wiki/Recettear:_An_Item_Shop%27s_Tale "Recettear: An Item Shop's Tale")*, the first such doujin to be published on Steam in 2010.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-escapist_steam-107)

Mikhail Fiadotau, a lecturer in video game studies at [Tallinn University](/wiki/Tallinn_University "Tallinn University"), identified three primary distinctions between the established doujin culture and the Western idea of indie games. From a conceptual view, indie games generally promote independence and novelty in thought, while doujin games tend to be ideas shared by a common group of people and tend to not veer from established concepts (such as strong favoritism towards the well-established RPG genre). From a genealogical standpoint, the nature of [doujin](/wiki/Doujin "Doujin") dates back as far as the 19th century, while the indie phenomena is relatively new. Finally, only until recently, doujin games tended to only be talked about in the same circles as other doujin culture (fan artwork and writing) and rarely mixed with commercial productions, whereas indie games have shared the same stage with AAA games.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Development")]

Main article: [Video game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development")

Many of the same basic concepts behind video game development for mainstream titles also apply to indie game development, particularly around the [software](/wiki/Software "Software") [development](/wiki/Software_development "Software development") aspects. Key differences lie in how the development of the game ties in with the publisher or lack thereof.

### Development teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Development teams")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/And_Yet_It_Moves_screenshot_02_walking_on_nothing.png/250px-And_Yet_It_Moves_screenshot_02_walking_on_nothing.png)](/wiki/File:And_Yet_It_Moves_screenshot_02_walking_on_nothing.png)

*[And Yet It Moves](/wiki/And_Yet_It_Moves "And Yet It Moves")* is an example of a student-developed game expanded to a commercial title following the team's graduation.

There is no definitive size for how big an independent game development studio might be. Several successful indie games, such as the *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")* series, *[Axiom Verge](/wiki/Axiom_Verge "Axiom Verge")*, *[Cave Story](/wiki/Cave_Story "Cave Story")*, *[Papers, Please](/wiki/Papers,_Please "Papers, Please")*, and *[Spelunky](/wiki/Spelunky "Spelunky")*, were developed by a single person, though often with support of artists and musicians for those assets.[[110]](#cite_note-110) More common are small teams of developers, from two to a few dozen, with additional support from external artists. While it is possible for development teams to be larger, with this comes a higher cost overhead of running the studio, which may be risky if the game does not perform well.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Indie teams can arise from many different directions. One common path recently includes student projects, developed as prototypes as part of their coursework, which the students then take into a commercial opportunity after graduating from school. Examples of such games are *[And Yet It Moves](/wiki/And_Yet_It_Moves "And Yet It Moves")*,[[112]](#cite_note-112) *[Octodad: Dadliest Catch](/wiki/Octodad:_Dadliest_Catch "Octodad: Dadliest Catch")*,[[113]](#cite_note-113) *[Risk of Rain](/wiki/Risk_of_Rain "Risk of Rain")*,[[114]](#cite_note-114) and *[Outer Wilds](/wiki/Outer_Wilds "Outer Wilds")*.[[115]](#cite_note-115) In some cases, students may drop out of school to pursue the commercial opportunity or for other reasons; [Vlambeer](/wiki/Vlambeer "Vlambeer")'s founders, for example, had started to develop a commercial game while still in school and dropped out when the school demanded rights to the game.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

Another route for indie development teams comes from experienced developers in the industry who either voluntarily leave to pursue indie projects, typically due to creative burnout from the corporate process, or resulting from termination from the company. Examples of games from such groups include *[FTL: Faster Than Light](/wiki/FTL:_Faster_Than_Light "FTL: Faster Than Light")*,[[117]](#cite_note-117) *[Papers, Please](/wiki/Papers,_Please "Papers, Please")*,[[118]](#cite_note-118) *[Darkest Dungeon](/wiki/Darkest_Dungeon "Darkest Dungeon")*,[[119]](#cite_note-119) and *[Gone Home](/wiki/Gone_Home "Gone Home")*.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Yet another route is simply those with little to no experience in the games industry, although they may have computer-programming skills and experience, and they may come in with ideas and fresh perspectives for games, with ideas that are generally more personable and close to their hearts. These developers are usually self-taught and thus may not have certain disciplines of typical programmers, thereby allowing for more creative freedom and new ideas.[[121]](#cite_note-121) However, some may see amateur work less favorably than those that have had experience, whether from school or from the industry, relying on game development toolkits rather than programming languages, and they may associate such titles as amateur or hobbyist.[[122]](#cite_note-122) Some such amateur-developed games have found great success. Examples of these include *[Braid](/wiki/Braid_(video_game) "Braid (video game)")*,[[123]](#cite_note-mtv_interview-123) *[Super Meat Boy](/wiki/Super_Meat_Boy "Super Meat Boy")*,[[124]](#cite_note-GSpostmortem1-124) *[Dwarf Fortress](/wiki/Dwarf_Fortress "Dwarf Fortress")*,[[125]](#cite_note-125) and *[Undertale](/wiki/Undertale "Undertale")*.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

Typically, a starting indie-game studio will be primarily programmers and developers. Art assets including artwork and music may be outsourced to work-for-hire artists and composers.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

### Development tools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Development tools")]

For development of personal computer games, indie games typically rely on existing [game engines](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine"), [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware") and [game development kits](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit") to build their titles, lacking the resources to build custom engines.[[27]](#cite_note-every_game_indie-27) Common game engines include [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") and [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(game_engine) "Unity (game engine)"), but there are numerous others as well. Small studios that do not anticipate large sales are generally afforded reduced prices for mainstream game engines and middleware. These products may be offered free, or be offered at a substantial royalty discount that only increases if their sales exceed certain numbers.[[128]](#cite_note-lipkin-128) Indie developers may also use [open source software](/wiki/Open_source_software "Open source software") (such as [Godot](/wiki/Godot_(game_engine) "Godot (game engine)")) or by taking advantage of [homebrew](/wiki/Homebrew_(video_games) "Homebrew (video games)") libraries, which are freely available but may lack technically advanced features compared to similar commercial engines.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

Prior to 2010, development of indie games on consoles was highly restrictive due to costly access to [software development kits](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit") (SDKs), typically a version of the console with added debugging features that would cost several thousands of dollars and come with numerous restrictions on its use to prevent trade secrets related to the console from being leaked. Console manufacturers may have also restricted sales of SDKs to only certain developers that met specific criteria, leaving potential indie developers unable to acquire them.[[132]](#cite_note-132) When indie games became more popular by 2010, the console manufacturers as well as mobile device operating system providers released special software-based SDKs to build and test games first on personal computers and then on these consoles or mobile devices. These SDKs were still offered at commercial rates to larger developers, but reduced pricing was provided to those who would generally self-publish via digital distribution on the console or mobile device's storefront, such as with the [ID@Xbox](/wiki/ID@Xbox "ID@Xbox") program or the [iOS SDK](/wiki/IOS_SDK "IOS SDK").

### Publishers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Publishers")]

While most indie games lack a publisher with the developer serving in that role, a number of publishers geared towards indie games have been established since 2010, also known as boutique game publishers; these include [Raw Fury](/wiki/Raw_Fury "Raw Fury"), [Devolver Digital](/wiki/Devolver_Digital "Devolver Digital"), [Annapurna Interactive](/wiki/Annapurna_Interactive "Annapurna Interactive"), Finji, and [Adult Swim Games](/wiki/Adult_Swim_Games "Adult Swim Games"). There also have been a number of indie developers that have grown large enough on their own to also support publishing for smaller developers, such as [Chucklefish](/wiki/Chucklefish "Chucklefish"), [Coffee Stain Studios](/wiki/Coffee_Stain_Studios "Coffee Stain Studios"), and [Team17](/wiki/Team17 "Team17"). These boutique publishers, having experience in making indie games themselves, typically will provide necessary financial support and marketing but have little to no creative control on developers' product as to maintain the "indie" nature of the game. In some cases, the publisher may be more selective of the type of games it supports; Annapurna Interactive sought games that were "personal, emotional and original".[[66]](#cite_note-gibiz_boutique-66)[[133]](#cite_note-arstech_funding-133)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Funding")]

The lack of a publisher requires an indie developer to find means to fund the game themselves. Existing studios may be able to rely on past funds and incoming revenue, but new studios may need to use their own personal funds ("bootstrapping"), personal or bank loans, or investments to cover development costs,[[12]](#cite_note-GameSpotRiseOfIndieDev-12)[[133]](#cite_note-arstech_funding-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134) or building community support while in development.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-gamasutra_fund_indie-136)

More recently, [crowd-funding](/wiki/Crowd-funding "Crowd-funding") campaigns, both reward-based and equity-based, have been used to obtain the funds from interested consumers before development begins in earnest. While [using crowd-funding for video games](/wiki/Crowdfunding_in_video_games "Crowdfunding in video games") took off in 2012, its practice has significantly waned as consumers became wary of campaigns that failed to deliver on promised goods. A successful crowd-funded campaign now typically requires significant development work and costs associated with this before the campaign is launched, in order to demonstrate that the game will likely be completed in a timely manner and draw in funds.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

Another mechanism offered through digital distribution is the [early access](/wiki/Early_access "Early access") model, in which interested players can buy playable [beta versions](/wiki/Beta_version "Beta version") of the game to provide software testing and gameplay feedback. Those consumers become entitled to the full game for free on release, while others may have to pay a higher price for the final game. This can provide funding midway though development, but like with crowd-funding, consumers expect a game that is near completion, so significant development and costs will likely need to have been invested already.[[138]](#cite_note-138) *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")* was considered an indie game during its original development, and was one of the first titles to successfully demonstrate this approach to funding.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

More recently, a number of dedicated investor-based indie game funds have been established such as the [Indie Fund](/wiki/Indie_Fund "Indie Fund"). Indie developers can submit applications requesting grants from these funds. The money is typically provided as a [seed investment](/wiki/Seed_investment "Seed investment") to be repaid through game royalties.[[136]](#cite_note-gamasutra_fund_indie-136) Several national governments, through their [public arts](/wiki/Public_art "Public art") agencies, also have made similar grants available to indie developers.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

### Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Distribution")]

Prior to digital distribution, hobbyist programmers typically relied on [mail order](/wiki/Mail_order "Mail order") to distribute their product. They would place ads in local papers or hobbyist computer magazines such as *[Creative Computing](/wiki/Creative_Computing_(magazine) "Creative Computing (magazine)")* and *[Byte](/wiki/Byte_(magazine) "Byte (magazine)")* and, once payment was received, fulfill orders by hand, making copies of their game to cassette tape, floppy disc, or CD-ROM along with documentation. Others would provide copies to their local computer store to sell. In the United Kingdom, where personal computer game development took off in the early 1980s, a market developed for game distributors that handled the copying and distribution of games for these hobbyist programmers.[[49]](#cite_note-wired_uk_history-49) In Japan, doujinshi conventions like [Comiket](/wiki/Comiket "Comiket"), the largest fan convention in the world, have allowed independent developers to sell and promote their physical products since its inauguration in 1975, allowing game series like *Touhou Project* and *[Fate](/wiki/Fate/stay_night "Fate/stay night")* to spread in popularity and dominate the convention for years.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143)

As the media shifted to higher-capacity formats and with the ability for users to make their own copies of programs, the simple mail order method was threatened since one person could buy the game and then make copies for their friends. The [shareware](/wiki/Shareware "Shareware") model of distribution emerged in the 1980s accepting that users would likely make copies freely and share these around. The shareware version of the software would be limited, and require payment to the developer to unlock the remaining features. This approach became popular with hobbyist games in the early 1990s, notably with the releases of *[Wolfenstein 3D](/wiki/Wolfenstein_3D "Wolfenstein 3D")* and *[ZZT](/wiki/ZZT "ZZT")*, "indie" games from fledgling developers [id Software](/wiki/Id_Software "Id Software") and [Tim Sweeney](/wiki/Tim_Sweeney_(game_developer) "Tim Sweeney (game developer)") (later founder of [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games")), respectively. Game magazines started to include shareware games on pack-in demo discs with each issue, and as with mail-order, companies arose that provided shareware sampler discs and served to help with shareware payment and redemption processing. Shareware remained a popular form of distribution even with availability of [bulletin board systems](/wiki/Bulletin_board_system "Bulletin board system") and the Internet.[[144]](#cite_note-144) By the 2000s, indie developers relied on the Internet as their primary distribution means as without a publisher, it was nearly impossible to stock an indie game at retail, the mail order concept having long since died out.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuireJenkins200927-42)

Continued Internet growth led to dedicated video game sites that served as repositories for shareware and other games, indie and mainstream alike, such as *[GameSpy](/wiki/GameSpy "GameSpy")*'s *FilePlanet*.[[145]](#cite_note-145) A new issue had arisen for larger mainstream games that featured multiplayer elements, in that updates and patches could easily be distributed through these sites but making sure all users were equally informed of the updates was difficult, and without the updates, some players would be unable to participate in multiplayer modes. [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation") built the [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") software client originally to serve these updates automatically for their games, but over time, it became a digital storefront that users could also purchase games through.[[146]](#cite_note-146) For indie games, Steam started curating third-party titles (including some indies) onto the service by 2005, later adding Steam Greenlight in 2012 that allowed any developer to propose their game for addition onto the service to the userbase, and ultimately replacing Greenlight with Steam Direct in 2017 where any developer can add their game to the service for a small fee.

While Steam remains the largest digital storefront for personal computer distribution, a number of other storefronts have since opened. For example, [Itch.io](/wiki/Itch.io "Itch.io"), established in 2013, has been more focused on serving indie games over mainstream ones, providing the developers with store pages and other tools to help with marketing. Other services act more as digital retailers, giving tools to the indie developer to be able to accept and redeem online purchases and distribute the game, such as [Humble Bundle](/wiki/Humble_Bundle "Humble Bundle"), but otherwise leaving the marketing to the developer.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

On consoles, the distribution of an indie game is handled by the console's game store, once the developer has been approved by the console manufacturer. Similarly, for mobile games, the distribution of the game is handled by the app store provider once the developer has been approved to release apps on that type of device. In either case, all aspects of payment, redemption and distribution are handled at the manufacturer/app store provider level.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

A recent trend for some of the more popular indies is a limited physical release, typical for console-based versions. The distributor [Limited Run Games](/wiki/Limited_Run_Games "Limited Run Games") was formed to produce limited runs of games, most commonly successful indie titles that have a proven following that would have a market for a physical edition. These versions are typically produced as [special editions](/wiki/Special_edition "Special edition") with additional physical products like art books, stickers, and other small items in the game's case. Other such distributors include [Super Rare Games](/wiki/Super_Rare_Games "Super Rare Games"), [Special Reserve Games](/wiki/Special_Reserve_Games "Special Reserve Games"), and [Strictly Limited Games](/w/index.php?title=Strictly_Limited_Games&action=edit&redlink=1 "Strictly Limited Games (page does not exist)").

In nearly all cases with digital distribution, the distribution platform takes a revenue cut of each sale with the rest of the sale going to the developer, as a means to pay for the costs of maintaining the digital storefront.

## Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Industry")]

Most indie games do not make a significant profit, and only a handful have made large profits.[[149]](#cite_note-GamasutraFlop-149) Instead, indie games are generally seen as a career stepping stone rather than a commercial opportunity.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIuppaBorst200910-52) The [Dunning–Kruger effect](/wiki/Dunning%E2%80%93Kruger_effect "Dunning–Kruger effect") has been shown to apply to indie games: some people with little experience have been able to develop successful games from the start, but for most, it takes upwards of ten years of experience within the industry before one regularly starts making games with financial success. Most in the industry caution that indie games should not be seen a financially-rewarding career for this reason.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

The industry perception towards indie games have also shifted, making the tactics of how to develop and market indie games difficult in contrast to AAA games. In 2008, a developer could earn around 17% of a game's retail price, and around 85% if sold digitally.[[32]](#cite_note-ForbesIndieDevsRiseUp-32) This can lead to the appearance of more "risky" creative projects.[[32]](#cite_note-ForbesIndieDevsRiseUp-32) Furthermore, the expansion of [social websites](/wiki/Social_network_service "Social network service") has introduced gaming to [casual gamers](/wiki/Casual_gamer "Casual gamer").[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3) Recent years have brought the importance of drawing social media influencers to help promote indie games as well.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

There is contention as to how prominent indie video game development is in the video game industry.[[28]](#cite_note-GamasutraFuture-28) Most games are not widely known or successful, and [mainstream media](/wiki/Mainstream_media "Mainstream media") attention remains with mainstream titles.[[152]](#cite_note-GamasutraBuzz-152)[[3]](#cite_note-GamasutraStateOfIndie-3) This can be attributed to a lack of marketing for indie games,[[152]](#cite_note-GamasutraBuzz-152) but indie games can be targeted at [niche markets](/wiki/Niche_market "Niche market").[[9]](#cite_note-GameTunnelInnovation-9)[[31]](#cite_note-TechradarGamingFuture-31)

Industry recognition of indie games through awards has grown significantly over time. The [Independent Games Festival](/wiki/Independent_Games_Festival "Independent Games Festival") was established in 1998 to recognize the best of indie games, and since its first event in 1999 has been held in conjunction with the [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") in the first part of each year alongside the [Game Developers Choice Awards](/wiki/Game_Developers_Choice_Awards "Game Developers Choice Awards") (GDCA).[[153]](#cite_note-153) However, it was not until 2010 when indie games were seen as similar competition to major gaming awards, with the 2010 GDCA recognizing games like *Limbo*, *Minecraft*, and *Super Meat Boy* among AAA titles.[[154]](#cite_note-gdca2011-154) Since then, indie games have frequently been included in award nominations alongside AAA games in the major awards events like the GDCA, the D.I.C.E. Awards, [The Game Awards](/wiki/The_Game_Awards "The Game Awards"), and the BAFTA Video Games Awards. Indie games like *[What Remains of Edith Finch](/wiki/What_Remains_of_Edith_Finch "What Remains of Edith Finch")*, *Outer Wilds*, *Untitled Goose Game*, *[Hades](/wiki/Hades_(video_game) "Hades (video game)")*, *[Inscryption](/wiki/Inscryption "Inscryption")*, and *[Vampire Survivors](/wiki/Vampire_Survivors "Vampire Survivors")* have been awarded various Game of the Year awards.[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

## Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Community")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Game_Developers_Conference_2013_-_Independent_Games_Festival_Expo.jpg/250px-Game_Developers_Conference_2013_-_Independent_Games_Festival_Expo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Game_Developers_Conference_2013_-_Independent_Games_Festival_Expo.jpg)

The [Independent Games Festival](/wiki/Independent_Games_Festival "Independent Games Festival") exhibition during the 2013 [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") in San Francisco, California, where indie developers can showcase their games to attendees

Indie developers are generally considered a highly collaborative community with development teams sharing knowledge between each other, providing testing, technical support, and feedback, as generally indie developers are not in any direct competition with each other once they have achieved funding for their project. Indie developers also tend to be open with their target player community, using beta testing and early access to get feedback, and engaging users regularly through storefront pages and communication channels such as [Discord](/wiki/Discord_(software) "Discord (software)").[[161]](#cite_note-161)

Indie game developers can be involved with various indie game [trade shows](/wiki/Trade_Shows "Trade Shows"), such as [Independent Games Festival](/wiki/Independent_Games_Festival "Independent Games Festival"), held alongside the [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference"), and [IndieCade](/wiki/IndieCade "IndieCade") held prior to the annual [E3](/wiki/E3 "E3") convention.[[2]](#cite_note-IGMWhatIsIndie-2)[[162]](#cite_note-162) The [Indie Megabooth](/wiki/Indie_Megabooth "Indie Megabooth") was established in 2012 as a large showcase at various trade shows to allow indie developers to show off their titles. These events act as intermediaries between indie developers and the larger industry, as they allow for indie developers to connect with larger developers and publishers for business opportunities, as well as to get word of their games out to the press prior to release.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

[Game jams](/wiki/Game_jam "Game jam"), including [Ludum Dare](/wiki/Ludum_Dare "Ludum Dare"), the [Indie Game Jam](/wiki/Indie_Game_Jam "Indie Game Jam"), the [Nordic Game Jam](/wiki/Nordic_Game_Jam "Nordic Game Jam"), and the [Global Game Jam](/wiki/Global_Game_Jam "Global Game Jam"), are typically annual competitions in which game developers are given a theme, concept and/or specific requirements and given a limited amount of time, on the order of a few days, to come up with a game prototype to submit for review and voting by judges, with the potential to win small cash prizes.[[164]](#cite_note-GamasutraTechInspCreative-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompsonBerbank-GreenCusworth200783-167) Companies can also have internal game jams as a means to relieve stress which may generate ideas for future games, as has notably been the case for developer [Double Fine](/wiki/Double_Fine "Double Fine") and its Amnesia Fortnight game jams. The structure of such jams can influence whether the end games are more experimental or serious, and whether they are to be more playful or more expressive.[[168]](#cite_note-168) While many game jam prototypes go no further, some developers have subsequently expanded the prototype into a full release after the game jam into successful indie games, such as *[Superhot](/wiki/Superhot "Superhot")*, *[Super Time Force](/wiki/Super_Time_Force "Super Time Force")*, *[Gods Will Be Watching](/wiki/Gods_Will_Be_Watching "Gods Will Be Watching")*, *[Hollow Knight](/wiki/Hollow_Knight "Hollow Knight")*, *[Surgeon Simulator](/wiki/Surgeon_Simulator "Surgeon Simulator")*, and *[Goat Simulator](/wiki/Goat_Simulator "Goat Simulator")*.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

## Impact and popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_game&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Impact and popularity")]

Indie games are recognized for helping to generate or revitalize [video game genres](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre"), either bringing new ideas to stagnant gameplay concepts or creating whole new experiences. The expansion of [roguelikes](/wiki/Roguelike "Roguelike") from [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII"), tile-based hack-and-slash games to a wide variety of so-called "rogue-lites" that maintain the roguelike procedural generation and permadeath features bore out directly from indie games *[Strange Adventures in Infinite Space](/wiki/Strange_Adventures_in_Infinite_Space "Strange Adventures in Infinite Space")* (2002) and its sequel *[Weird Worlds: Return to Infinite Space](/wiki/Weird_Worlds:_Return_to_Infinite_Space "Weird Worlds: Return to Infinite Space")* (2005), *[Spelunky](/wiki/Spelunky "Spelunky")* (2008), *[The Binding of Isaac](/wiki/The_Binding_of_Isaac_(video_game) "The Binding of Isaac (video game)")* (2011), *[FTL: Faster Than Light](/wiki/FTL:_Faster_Than_Light "FTL: Faster Than Light")* (2012) and *[Rogue Legacy](/wiki/Rogue_Legacy "Rogue Legacy")* (2012).[[170]](#cite_note-170) In turn, new takes on the roguelike genre were inspired by *[Slay the Spire](/wiki/Slay_the_Spire "Slay the Spire")* (2019), which popularized the [roguelike deck-building game](/wiki/Roguelike_deck-building_game "Roguelike deck-building game"),[[171]](#cite_note-171) and *[Vampire Survivors](/wiki/Vampire_Survivors "Vampire Survivors")* (2022), which led to numerous "[bullet heaven](/wiki/Bullet_heaven "Bullet heaven")" or reverse [bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell") games using roguelike mechanics.[[172]](#cite_note-172) [Metroidvanias](/wiki/Metroidvania "Metroidvania") resurged following the releases of *[Cave Story](/wiki/Cave_Story "Cave Story")* (2004) and *[Shadow Complex](/wiki/Shadow_Complex "Shadow Complex")* (2009).[[105]](#cite_note-Nutt-105) *[Stardew Valley](/wiki/Stardew_Valley "Stardew Valley")* (2016) created a resurgence in [life simulation games](/wiki/Life_simulation_game "Life simulation game").[[173]](#cite_note-173) [Art games](/wiki/Art_game "Art game") have gained attention through indie developers with early indie titles such as *[Samorost](/wiki/Samorost "Samorost")* (2003)[[174]](#cite_note-ploug-174) and *[The Endless Forest](/wiki/The_Endless_Forest "The Endless Forest")* (2005).[[175]](#cite_note-175)
